text: Single-cell transcriptomics has broadened our understanding of cellular diversity and gene expression dynamics in healthy and diseased tissues. Recently, spatial transcriptomics has emerged as a tool to contextualize single cells in multicellular neighbourhoods and to identify spatially recurrent phenotypes, or ecotypes. These technologies have generated vast datasets with targeted-transcriptome and whole-transcriptome profiles of hundreds to millions of cells. Such data have provided new insights into developmental hierarchies, cellular plasticity and diverse tissue microenvironments, and spurred a burst of innovation in computational methods for single-cell analysis. In this Review, we discuss recent advancements, ongoing challenges and prospects in identifying and characterizing cell states and multicellular neighbourhoods. We discuss recent progress in sample processing, data integration, identification of subtle cell states, trajectory modelling, deconvolution and spatial analysis. Furthermore, we discuss the increasing application of deep learning, including foundation models, in analysing single-cell and spatial transcriptomics data. Finally, we discuss recent applications of these tools in the fields of stem cell biology, immunology, and tumour biology, and the future of single-cell and spatial transcriptomics in biological research and its translation to the clinic.

Since its inception nearly 15 years ago, single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq) has transformed our fundamental understanding of cellular heterogeneity and gene expression dynamics across various species, tissues and stages of life. This methodology has enabled the construction of large cell atlases, discovery of rare cell types and states, and mapping of cell trajectories during complex biological processes such as early embryogenesis, brain development and cancer growth. RNA can be profiled from individual cells at an unprecedented scale and resolution, integrated with genomic and proteomic assays, and measured in situ to reveal the intricate spatial organization and functional relationships between neighbouring cells within tissues. However, technical challenges arise from sample preparation, dissociation, and batch effects. Tissue dissociation can induce transcriptional changes, such as stress responses or apoptosis, altering cellular composition. Single-nucleus RNA-seq (snRNA-seq) is an alternative for frozen or difficult-to-dissociate tissues but excludes key cytoplasmic transcripts. Chemical fixation preserves tissue context but corrupts ultrastructure, limiting whole-transcriptome sequencing.

In single-cell experiments involving multiple samples, biological differences can be obscured by technical covariates. Computational methods have been developed to correct batch-specific variation while preserving biological signal. ComBat and limma are linear models used for bulk data but face challenges with scRNA-seq sparsity. Methods like Mutual Nearest Neighbours (MNN) identify neighbourhoods of similar cells across batches to integrate data. Dimensionality reduction techniques such as PCA (e.g., fastMNN, BBKNN, Harmony), Singular Value Decomposition (e.g., Scanorama), and Non-negative Matrix Factorization (e.g., LIGER) find a common biological space. Seurat uses Canonical Correlation Analysis (CCA). Pseudobulk analysis aggregates gene expression to treat samples as replicates, minimizing sparsity. Semi-supervised deep learning methods like scANVI and scGEN outperform unsupervised methods in preserving biological variation during batch correction.

Massive efforts are underway to construct annotated cell atlases across tissues and species. Challenges include data integration across variable processing and establishing a unified cell state ontology. Strategies for identifying rare and subtle cell states include using plate-based full-length sequencing (e.g., MAS-seq, VASA-seq) which offers deeper RNA capture and lower dropout rates compared to droplet-based methods. Feature selection is critical; methods like GiniClust (Gini coefficient), CellSIUS (bimodal gene sets), and scPNMF (projective NMF) identify genes distinguishing rare states. Deep generative models like scLDS learn distributions and infer rare cells. Reference atlases allow annotation transfer via correlation-based (e.g., SingleR), classifier-based (e.g., scPred), or large language model (e.g., GPTCelltype) approaches.

Trajectory inference tools delineate transcriptional paths during dynamic processes. RNA velocity estimates transition rates by comparing pre-mRNA to mature mRNA; protaccel adds protein velocity. Lineage tracing uses engineered (e.g., Polylox, TraceSeq) or natural (e.g., mtDNA variants) barcodes. Cellular plasticity, the potential to adopt different fates, is modelled probabilistically using Markov chains (e.g., Palantir, VIA, MARGARET) or Gaussian processes (e.g., GpFates, STEMNET). CellRank uses ensemble approaches with RNA velocity and CytoTRACE. Developmental potential, measuring differentiation capacity, is assessed via transcriptional programmes (e.g., ORIGINs, mRNAsi, PluriNet) or surrogate measures like transcriptional diversity and entropy (e.g., CytoTRACE, SCENT), which correlate with genome-wide chromatin accessibility.

ST preserves tissue context, characterizing spatial arrangements. Technologies vary in resolution: 'bulk ST' (e.g., Visium, Slide-seq, Stereo-seq) uses spatial barcoding with spot sizes from 55μm down to 10μm, while probe/image-based methods (e.g., MERFISH, Xenium, CosMx) achieve subcellular resolution but with limited gene panels. Computational tools address limitations: deconvolution methods (e.g., Cell2Location, RCTD, SpatialDWLS) estimate cell type proportions in spots; others (e.g., Tangram, CellTrek, CytoSPACE) map scRNA-seq cells to spatial coordinates. Defining multicellular neighbourhoods (fundamental units of ST analysis) uses fixed radii, k-nearest neighbours, or Delaunay triangulation. Representations include cell type composition or aggregated gene expression features.

Spatial ecotypes are recurrent groups of spatially colocalized and phenotypically coherent cell states. They are identified by correlating cell state frequencies across samples or regions. Deep learning, particularly geometric deep learning (e.g., GraphST, SpaceFlow, CCST), enables embedding of multicellular neighbourhoods. Foundation models (e.g., scGPT, SCimilarity, GeneFormer) trained on large atlases create latent spaces for cell embeddings, enabling tasks like cell type annotation, batch integration, and perturbation prediction. Challenges remain in model interpretability and ensuring biological signal capture rather than dataset biases. Digital cytometry uses reference profiles to deconvolute bulk RNA-seq, translating single-cell findings to large clinical cohorts.
